title: After using macOS pkg installer `/opt/podman/bin` is not available in my USER path
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug


**Description**

Using the macOS installer on a fresh macOS installation, I run the install flow, it's successfully completing but at the end, when opening a terminal I don't have `podman` being available

Default shell is zsh (fresh install)

I think the issue is because we check if we have some .rc/.env file for a given shell before appending the path to one of this file but as we don't have one, it does nothing

https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/contrib/pkginstaller/scripts/postinstall


**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Use a fresh macOS install, no .rc/env shell stuff

2. Install the .pkg

3. Open a terminal and launch `podman` command

**Describe the results you received:**

podman not found


**Describe the results you expected:**

podman binary is there


**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
(paste your output here)
```

**Output of `podman info`:**

```
(paste your output here)
```

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

```
(paste your output here)
```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide? (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md)**


Yes/No

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**


![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/436777/190600915-340b07bb-527f-43a5-9e47-4995d66c5f2d.png)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/436777/190600971-74dd8ea4-9851-4226-bf76-ad24105441ca.png)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/436777/190601005-99c26d67-0d25-43c9-9a64-a4e5e92a996e.png)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/436777/190601039-17c5b0a9-7950-4dfa-a59a-b71ef4634681.png)

comments.body:
```
% cat /etc/paths.d/podman
/opt/podman/bin
```
should do the trick  (see `man path_helper`) and you can remove most of the postinstall script. Since the installer could create `/opt/podman/bin`, I assume it should be able to write to `/etc/paths.d/podman` as well.

the issue is with the check

```bash
if [ -f "$PATH" ]; then
   ...
fi
```
in the current https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/ccee741973a182f65af4e58302c9a76107974f0a/contrib/pkginstaller/scripts/postinstall

the previous suggestion is better than ignoring the check, as we do not want to create files that might never be needed.

> Since the installer could create /opt/podman/bin, I assume it should be able to write to /etc/paths.d/podman as well.

Yes from installer you can able to write `/etc/paths.d/podman` locations.
